api-gateway / request handling

How request rate limiting works in the token-bucket gateway

By the end you'll understand the one fact that governs almost every rate-limit surprise: buckets are keyed per caller, not per route, and every decision downstream of that follows from it.

On this page

TL;DR Request path Configuration Gotchas FAQ

TL;DR

The gateway hands every caller a bucket of tokens that refills at a steady rate. Each request costs one token; if the bucket is empty, the request is rejected with a 429 before it ever reaches a handler. Buckets live in a shared store (not in gateway process memory), so the limit holds even when requests land on different gateway instances. The bucket is identified by caller — an API key or account id — not by which endpoint was called, which is why hitting five different routes drains the same bucket as hitting one route five times.

Request path

A client request enters the rate-limit middleware, which checks and decrements a token in the shared bucket store; if a token was available the request continues to the handler, otherwise the middleware short-circuits with a 429.

What happens, in order

  1. The middleware extracts the caller identity from the request (API key, falling back to account id) and derives the bucket key.
  2. It asks the bucket store for the current token count for that key, refilling first if time has elapsed since the last check.
  3. If at least one token is available, it decrements the count atomically and lets the request proceed to the handler.
  4. If the bucket is empty, it rejects the request immediately with a 429 and a Retry-After header, before any handler code runs.
  5. The handler executes with no knowledge that limiting happened — rate limiting is fully invisible past the middleware boundary.

Configuration

Limits are declared once per environment in a YAML file loaded at gateway startup and hot-reloaded on change.

config/limits.yaml
# token-bucket limits, keyed by plan tier
default:
  capacity: 120
  refill_rate: 2
  refill_unit: "second"

tiers:
  free:
    capacity: 60
    refill_rate: 1
  pro:
    capacity: 600
    refill_rate: 10

key_source: "header:x-api-key"
Field Default Meaning
capacity 120 Maximum tokens a bucket can hold — the burst size a caller can spend all at once.
refill_rate 2 / sec Tokens added back per refill_unit, up to capacity — the sustained steady-state rate.
key_source header Where the middleware reads the caller identity used as the bucket key.

The bucket is per-caller, not per-route — that single fact explains most surprises.

Gotchas

Note. Refill is lazy, not on a background timer — the store computes elapsed time and tops up the bucket only when a request actually checks it, so an idle caller's bucket is always "full" by the time they return.

Shared state. Because the bucket lives in the shared store rather than per-instance memory, a store outage fails open to unlimited traffic by design — confirm that's still the intended failure mode before changing it.

FAQ

Why did I get rate-limited hitting different endpoints?

Because the bucket key is the caller, not the route. Every endpoint you call under the same API key draws from the same bucket.

Does a burst of requests get rejected outright?

No — up to capacity tokens can be spent in a burst. Only requests beyond the current token count are rejected, and only until the next refill.

Can a single route override the caller's tier limit?

Not today. The middleware only looks up limits by tier in config/limits.yaml; per-route overrides would need a new key scheme in middleware/ratelimit.ts.

Sources: middleware/ratelimit.ts (token-bucket check and decrement logic), config/limits.yaml (tier and capacity configuration).
